author: @robertchristensen7950
text: Something else to note, is that urea supposedly will fall out of solution when there's a high concentration of ethanol. So you could use the bare minimum of water, than add a couple gallons of ethanol to make it crystallize back into urea crystals.

author: @markgeurts258
text: Hi Robert, I have done a experiment with some zeolith, and it is true that you can dry it in de sun, but only if you heat it up to 200°C/300°Celsius you will get the heat reaction with water. I've dried it last summer in my dry/hot greenhouse, and if you add some water it adsorbs really quickly but without the forming of heat!

author: @saifcathum3423
text: I got a hold of a Mercedes S-Class mild hybrid battery. The housing had what appeared to be Peltier elements based on the fact that applying voltage produced heat on one side and cold on the other. When I disassembled the housing I found thin metal elements sandwiched inside instead of the white ceramic plates I was expecting. I forgot I had them until now. I will have to do some experimenting with them after watching this.

author: @jimjohnson9200
text:
A few things!! As always, THANK YOU for doing what you do and sharing your knowledge and expertise! I enjoy them! (Although others listening in to me just roll their eyes🙄)
 I just turned the big 5 0. Grew up reading and watching Don Herbert, aka Mr. Wizard- same as my father before me. So I understand your language and thought processes. 
  Crazy enough, during this pandemic I've had the idle time to think and ponder on filtration of water and other liquids. Waste. Urine, urea, ammonia. Various clay types used as absorbance (cat boxes) and pinging there has got to be a better way of disposal for animal wastes...
  All of our kitchen scraps, lawn clippings, papers, etc are all recycled and composted.. so really, it's natural to see the "in and out" so to speak of our garden and what we grow, what we consume and what we "waste." What we recycle in terms of paper, metals, glass, and plastics.
  We use rain barrels, filtration for garden and general use. City tap filtered by osmosis for food prep and drinking.
 I appreciate your thinking. Your videos jog and massage that inner genius in me.. thank you again sir!

author: @idea-shack
text: Idea: You can use three peltiers in a stacked series, pure water (will form ice) on the one side and barium hydroxide octahydrate on the other side (will melt at 78C). Efficiency is terrible, but should make a nice demo for a reversible heat battery. Barium hydroxide octahydrate and water have high heats of fusion, so makes the thing more energy dense. Since the dT is constant, the voltage should also be constant until the system equilibrates.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice idea mate - but I do have another one I am planning on

replies.author: @idea-shack
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Cheers! Looking forward to seeing it!

author: @synchro-dentally1965
text: I had a professor mention in a lecture how often water plays a roll in energy generation/conversion. It seems to be involved in almost all types in one way or another.

author: @idea-shack
text: You can also make a reversible battery with this, if you use two solids that melt at different temperatures, salt hydrates work well, you just have to match a hot melting one and a cold melting one. When you charged it, you melt the hot melting hydrate and freeze the cold melting hydrate, then when you remove the voltage, heat flows from the hot melting hydrate as it freezes and melts the cold melting hydrate and eventually you reach an equilibrium, then you need to recharge. The nice thing about it is that you can cycle it as much as you like!!!
